#2nd Part Dhyāna: Ṣaḍaṅga yoga text by Tārānāthā

# \#2nd Part Dhyāna: Ṣaḍaṅga yoga text by Tārānāthā

[](https://substack.com/@tseringdorji)

![Tsering Dorji's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VB0T!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faedf658f-bb6c-4511-9227-fcdbd4e7c7bd_768x773.jpeg)

[Tsering Dorji](https://substack.com/@tseringdorji)

Mar 15, 2024

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

5

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

### Translator’s Note

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

- In this post you will find the Tibetan text and English translation of
  the second half of the *Dhyāna* Practice of *Ṣaḍaṅga Yoga* (Six limbs
  of yoga) text written by *Tārānāthā*, with explanations in brackets
  based on *Tārānāthā*'s own writings. Click here to read the [first
  part](https://open.substack.com/pub/tsering/p/dhyana-sadanga-yoga-text-composed?r=19pwkm&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web).

- Number of objects of purification (དག་བྱ་དབྱེ་ན་): There are six sets of
  six (36) objects of purification in general (in *pratyāhāra*, every
  sixth section of the groups is purified). They are: **1. Six
  aggregates** (*skandhas*): physical body, sensation, perception,
  formation, consciousness and primordial wisdom. **2. Six elements**
  (*dhatus*): earth, water, fire, air, space and wisdom element. **3.
  Six senses (***indriyā*): Eyes, ears, nose, tongue, body and mental
  faculty. **4. Six sense objects**: form, sound, smell, taste, touch
  and expanse of reality (*dharmadhatu*). **5. Six faculties of action**
  (*karmendriyāṇi*): throat, arms, legs, genitals, anus and divine
  faculty (*divyendriya*). **6. Six actions of the faculties of
  action**: speaking, fetching, walking, passing urine/semen/blood, and
  faeces.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mQGR!,w_5760,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F754654fc-abfb-4fb5-998e-5c0423a71b5d_1730x295.png)

### II. The meaning and purpose of the practice of Dhyāna

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> གཉིས་པ་སྒོམ་དོན་བློ་ལ་འགོད་ཚུལ་དྲུག
> ```

In the meaning and purpose of dhyāna practice, there are six things to remember: 1. Meaning of the term dhyāna. 2. When to practice. 3. Ways of practising. 4. Signs of the accomplishment of the practice. 5. The object of purification, the purifier and their classification. 6. Fruits of the accomplishment of dhyāna practice.

#### 1. Meaning of the term *dhyāna*

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །བསམ་གཏན་མཚན་དོན་དྷྱཱ་ན་ཞེས་པ་ནི། 
> །དམིགས་པར་སེམས་ནི་གཡོ་བ་མེད་པའམ།
> །དམིགས་པ་དབང་བར་བྱེད་པའི་དོན་ཡིན་ཏེ། 
> །རྣམ་ཀུན་མཆོག་ལྡན་སྟོང་གཟུགས་འོད་གསལ་ལ། 
> །རྩེ་གཅིག་གནས་དང་འགྱུ་བ་དབང་ཐོབ་པའི། 
> །དོན་ཏེ་སྤྱི་ལྡོག་བསམ་གཏན་རྩེ་གཅིག་པ།
> ```

The meaning of the term dhyāna is to keep the mind fixed on its object or to gain mastery over the object (being able to keep the mind on or off the object at will). This means that one is able to focus one's mind on and off the luminous image of emptiness, which is endowed with the most sublime of qualities, at will. The common meaning of dhyāna (for all vehicles/yāna) is one-pointedness.

#### 2. When to practice

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །གཉིས་པ་སྒོམ་པའི་དུས་མཚམས་སོར་སྡུད་ནི། 
> །མཐར་ཕྱིན་རབ་སྟེ་མིན་ནའང་འོད་གསལ་གྱི། 
> །རྟགས་སྣང་བརྟན་པོར་ཤར་བའི་དུས་སུ་སྒོམ།
> ```

If you can practice dhyāna when you have accomplished pratyāhāra, it is excellent. If not, practise when a stable vision of signs of luminosity (of image of emptiness) arises.

#### 3. Ways of practising and the five limbs of *Dhyāna*

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> གསུམ་པ་སྒོམ་ཚུལ་ཡན་ལག་བསམ་གཏན་གྱི། 
> །ངོ་བོ་འོད་གསལ་རྟགས་སྣང་རྩེ་གཅིག་ཏུ། 
> །དམིགས་པའི་ཏིང་འཛིན་དེ་ཡི་ཡན་ལག་ལྔ།
> ```

Samādhi that is one-pointedly focused on the vision of the signs of luminosity (the image of emptiness) is the nature of dhyāna. There are five limbs of dhyāna.

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །རྟགས་བཅུ་རྩེ་གཅིག་སེམས་ནི་ཤེས་རབ་ཡིན། 
> །སྟོང་གཟུགས་མཚན་ཉིད་ཤེས་པ་རྟོག་པ་ཡིན། 
> །མཚན་ཉིད་ཤེས་ཚུལ་རང་སེམས་ཉམས་སུ་མྱོང་། 
> །སེམས་ཉིད་སྟོང་གཟུགས་ཤར་བ་སྤྲོས་མཚན་མེད། 
> །མྱོང་ཞིང་ངེས་པ་དཔྱོད་པའི་ཡན་ལག་ཡིན། 
> །ཏིང་འཛིན་སྟོབས་གྱིས་དགའ་སྐྱིད་བདེ་ཡན་ལག 
> །ཏིང་འཛིན་བདེ་མྱོང་གཡོ་མེད་བདེ་ཡན་ལག། 
> དེས་རྐྱེན་བྱས་ནས་ལུས་ལ་ཤིན་སྦྱངས་ཀྱི། 
> །བདེ་བས་ཁྱབ་པ་དེ་ཡི་ཕན་ཡོན་ནོ།
> ```

**(Five limbs of dhyāna are)**

(i) The one-pointed mind (that is focused) on the ten signs is the **wisdom** factor.

(ii) Understanding the characteristic of the image of emptiness is the ***thinking*** factor of dhyāna. The way to understand the characteristic of (the image of emptiness) is by experiencing (the image of emptiness ) as your own mind (or is not separate from your mind).

(iii) Experiencing (knowing with strong feeling) with certainty that the mind itself appears in the form of the image of emptiness without any attributes of (conceptual) fabrication (of the subject-object dichotomy) is the **examining** factor of dhyāna.

(iv) The happiness of joy (prītisukha) brought by Samādhi is the **joy and ease** factor of dhyāna.

(v) Experiencing the unwavering happiness (bliss) that Samādhi brings is the **bliss** factor of dhyāna. This (unwavering bliss factor) causes the bliss of tranquillity (praśrabdhi) to permeate the body. That is its beneficial effect.

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །དེ་ཡང་དང་པོར་ཤེས་རབ་རྐྱང་པ་སྐྱེ། 
> །དེ་ནས་གཉིས་ལྡན་གསུམ་ལྡན་བཞི་ལྡན་དང་། 
> །ལྔ་ལྡན་རིམ་འབྱུང་ཡན་ལག་རྫོགས་པར་སྐྱེས། 
> །རྒྱུན་བསྲིངས་བསྒོམས་ན་མཐར་ཕྱིན་ཡོན་ཏན་སྐྱེ།
> ```

First, only wisdom (factor) will arise. Then (the fruits of the limbs of dhyāna) will arise in stages, (next) twofold (wisdom and thinking factor), then threefold (wisdom, thinking and examining factor), then fourfold (wisdom, thinking, examining, and joy and ease). When the fivefold (wisdom, thinking, examining, joy and ease, and bliss) arise, then all the limbs of dhyāna will arise in your stream of being. If you constantly maintain this practice for long time, the accomplishments (positive effects) of perfection (of dhyāna) will arise.

#### 4. Signs of the accomplishment of the practice

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །བཞི་པ་བསྒོམ་པའི་རྟགས་ཚད་རྒྱུད་ཇི་བཞིན། 
> །སོར་སྡུད་ལས་ལྷག་རྟགས་བཅུས་བསྡུས་པ་ཡི། 
> །སྲིད་གསུམ་གཟུགས་བརྙན་འོད་ཟེར་འཕྲོ་བ་དང༌། 
> །ཕྲ་བ་འཚེར་བ་འདར་བ་གཞིག་དཀའ་བ། 
> །ལྔ་ལྡན་ཐིག་ལེ་དུ་མས་མཚན་པ་མཐོང་། 
> །ཐིག་ལེའི་དབུས་སུ་སངས་རྒྱས་དུ་མ་འཆར། 
> །ཀུན་རྫོབ་ལས་སྣང་སྒྱ་མ་ལྟ་བུར་འཆར།
> །ཚད་མ་སོར་སྡུད་དང་མཚུངས་མངོན་ཤེས་འགྲུབ།
> ```

The signs of the accomplishment of dhyāna are the same as those mentioned in Tantra. The reflection of the three worlds (the image of the emptiness of form, sound, smell, taste and touch) included in the ten signs seen during pratyāhāra is much greater in dhyāna. In dhyāna, the practitioner will see (the reflection of form, sound, smell, etc. and their colour) adorned with a multitude of bindu, emanating light, minute subtle part (sūkṣmam), shining, trembling and difficult to destroy. In the centre of the bindu many images of Buddha will appear. All the apparent reality of the karmic vision will appear as an illusion. The sense perceptions in pratyāhāra and dhyāna are both valid cognitions; on this basis they are equal. (However, in dhyāna the sense faculty is the divine eye. So) the practitioner attains the five super-knowledges (pañcābhijñāḥ) in dhyāna.

#### 5. The object of purification, the purifier and their classification

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །ལྔ་པ་དག་བྱ་དག་བྱེད་དབྱེ་བ་ལ། 
> །དག་གཞི་སོར་སྡུད་སྐབས་བཞིན་དག་བྱ་ལ། 
> །དབྱེ་ན་རྣམ་ཤེས་ཕུང་སོགས་མཁའ་ཁམས་དྲུག 
> །དག་བྱེད་དབྱེ་ན་བསམ་གཏན་ཡན་ལག་ལྔ། 
> །དེའི་ཡུལ་སྟོང་གཟུགས་ལྔ་སྟེ་བཅུ་ཡོད་དོ།
> ```

The basis of purification (of the defilements) in dhyāna is the same as in pratyāhāra (that is tathagatagarbha/Buddha nature). The object of purification is the fifth consciousness section of the six sets of aggregates and elements, such as the aggregate of consciousness (of the six aggregates), the element of space (of the six elements). As purifiers (that which purifies the defilements) there are ten, (subjective) five limbs of dhyāna and (objective) five images of emptiness.

#### 6. Fruits of the accomplishment of *dhyāna* practice

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །དྲུག་པ་བསྒོམ་པས་ཐོབ་པའི་འབྲས་བུ་ནི། 
> །གནས་སྐབས་འབྲས་བུ་མངོན་ཤེས་ལྔ་ཐོབ་ཞིང་། 
> །མཐར་ཐུག་འབྲས་བུ་རྒྱལ་བ་མི་བསྐྱོད་སོགས། 
> །མཁའ་ཁམས་དྲུག་ཚན་གཞི་དབྱིངས་མངོན་དུ་འགྱུར།
> ```

The fruit (of practising dhyāna) for the time being is the attainment of the five super-knowledges. Its ultimate fruit is the realisation of the deities of the fifth section of the six sets of six objects of purification, such as Akṣobhya.

#### Supplementary Explanation

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །འཕྲོས་དོན་མན་ངག་ཤེས་རབ་ཡན་ལག་སྐྱེས། 
> །སེམས་གང་གཏད་སར་སྟོང་གཟུགས་འཆར་བ་འབྱུང་།
> ```

Now the secret instruction of the supplementary explanation (is being taught). When (the first limb of dhyāna) wisdom arises (in you), (you) will be able to see the image of emptiness wherever you focus your mind.

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །དེ་ནས་སྣང་བའི་སྟེང་དུ་འོད་གསལ་འཛིན། 
> །ཕྱི་རོལ་སྣང་བ་གང་ལ་སེམས་བཞག་པ། 
> །ལས་སྣང་འགགས་ཏེ་འོད་གསལ་སྣང་འགྱུར་ན།
> ```

Then (practise the practice of) placing the luminosity (image of emptiness) over the karmic vision (such as visible object, sound, smell, etc.). (By practising this) whatever objects (the practitioner) puts their mind on, the karmic vision (ordinary form and sound, etc.) will cease and will only see the luminosity.

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །སྔ་དྲོ་ཕྱི་དྲོར་རང་ཉིད་གཅེར་བུར་ཕྱུང་། 
> །གྲིབ་གཟུགས་སྙིང་གར་ལྟ་བའམ་ཉིན་གུང་དུ། 
> །ལྗོན་ཤིང་གྲིབ་མར་ལྟ་བས་ཀྱང་རུང་སྟེ། 
> །མིག་སྦྲེད་སྦྲེད་དུ་གཡོ་བ་མེད་པར་ལྟ། 
> ```

(When the practitioner achieves this), in the morning and in the afternoon, stripped naked, gaze at the heart area of the shadow. It is also okay to look at the shadow of a tree at noon. (But) look without any movement (of your gaze) until your eyes become blurred (!?).

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །དེ་ནས་ནམ་མཁར་བལྟས་པས་གྲིབ་གཟུགས་ཀྱི།  
> །རྣམ་པའི་དབུས་སུ་རྟགས་སྣང་དཔག་མེད་འཆར།  
> །ཟླ་དྲུག་བསྒོམས་པས་ས་འོག་གཏེར་སོགས་མཐོང་།
> ```

(After you have mastered this) when you look at the sky, there will appear innumerable visionary signs in the midst of the shadowy form. If you practise this (over and over again) for about six months, you will be able to see the treasure under the ground.

> Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when
> published
>
> ``` text
> །དེ་བཞིན་སྐར་ཁུང་སོགས་སྦྱངས་ཁྱིམ་ནང་གི 
> །ལྐོག་གྱུར་མཐོང་སོགས་གཞན་ལས་ཤེས་པར་བྱ། 
> །དེས་ནི་བསམ་གཏན་འཁྲིད་རིམ་རྫོགས་པའོ།
> ```

Also practise on the window (small opening in a house to let light in) of your house. You will be able to see the hidden objects inside the house. Here is the end of the stages of instruction in dhyāna.

Tsering Dorji's Newsletter is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

5
